title: Hormuz Reopens in Theory, Not in Practice — While Markets Price a 2027 Oil Glut
subhead: The US-Iran framework deal says ships can move again, but demining, Iranian 'service fees,' and reluctant insurers mean the Strait won't return to normal for weeks. Meanwhile, the IEA sees a massive supply surplus looming in 2027, and the G7 moved on Russia's shadow fleet and China's rare earth dominance.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Deal Signed, Strait Still Blocked
sections.body:
The US-Iran interim peace deal announced at the G7 summit in Évian commits to reopening the Strait of Hormuz, but the waterway remains far from operational. Roughly 1,500 ships remain stuck inside the Persian Gulf, according to industry analysts cited by NPR, including hundreds of oil tankers. Before the war, about 140 vessels transited daily; current traffic via a de-mined southern corridor off Oman is running at just 25 ships per day, with a US official projecting it could reach 40–50 quickly — but full normalization could take up to 30 days.

Britain and France have committed to demining operations. UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer confirmed the deployment of autonomous mine-hunting drones, counter-drone systems, Typhoon jets, and the HMS Dragon. The US is using a mix of drone boats, helicopters, and warships for demining, according to military analysts. RAND's Scott Savitz assessed that demining could achieve 'an acceptable level of risk' given the high number of Iranian minelaying vessels destroyed.

The International Chamber of Shipping warned that merchant vessels will return gradually, citing the need for confirmation that transit lanes are mine-free and that the agreement is actually holding.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between a signed deal and a functional shipping lane is measured in weeks, not hours. Every day of delayed traffic extends the inventory drawdown and keeps oil prices elevated despite the ceasefire announcement.

sections.heading: Iran's 'Service Fees' — A Toll by Any Other Name
sections.body:
President Trump declared the Strait would reopen 'permanently toll-free,' but Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei immediately contradicted that framing, saying Iran was 'not seeking to levy transit tolls' but would charge 'fees' for 'services provided' — including navigation, environmental protection, and maritime support.

James R. Holmes, chair of maritime strategy at the US Naval War College, told the New York Times there is 'no provision in international law for a coastal state charging for passage through a natural waterway, whether you call it a toll or a fee or whatever.' The International Maritime Organization has also warned that charging for passage would set a 'dangerous precedent.'

The IRGC set up what it called a 'toll booth' in March; it remains unclear whether any ship owners paid. The fee-vs-toll distinction matters because US companies face sanctions exposure if they pay an entity connected to the IRGC, which Washington designates as a terrorist organization.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is testing whether it can extract revenue from Hormuz transit under a different label. If accepted, it creates a permanent toll on the world's most important shipping lane — and a precedent for other coastal states to follow.

sections.heading: IEA Forecasts Oil Glut in 2027 as Middle East Recovery Begins
sections.body:
The IEA's June Oil Market Report, released Wednesday, projects a 5.05 million barrel-per-day surplus in 2027 as Middle East production ramps back up. The war is estimated to have blocked more than 14 million bpd of Middle East output at its peak. Middle East flows have already recovered to roughly 12 million bpd in early June from a May low of 9.6 million bpd, driven by ship-to-ship transfers in the Gulf of Oman.

Global oil demand is forecast to fall by 1.1 million bpd in 2026 after a 5 million bpd April–June collapse, with demand destruction spreading beyond initially affected regions. OPEC separately cut its 2026 demand growth forecast to 970,000 bpd. Inventories have been drawn down at 3.8 million bpd since the war began on February 28, with May draws at 4.6 million bpd.

But the IEA warns that inventories could plunge further to historic lows before the surplus materializes toward end-2026. The implied 2026 supply deficit narrowed to 920,000 bpd from 1.78 million bpd in the previous month's report.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil prices are falling on the deal — Brent dropped to around $77.75, down over a third from April peaks — but the market faces a whipsaw: severe inventory draws now, then a potential 5 million bpd surplus next year. The speed of the Hormuz reopening determines how painful the gap period gets.

sections.heading: Canada Sanctions Russia's Shadow Fleet; G7 Considers New Oil and Gas Sanctions
sections.body:
Canadian PM Mark Carney announced new sanctions targeting Russia's shadow fleet, energy revenues, defense-industrial sector, and disinformation entities at the G7 summit, according to Reuters. The measures are the latest in a series of allied actions against the network of aging tankers Moscow uses to move oil outside Western oversight.

At the same time, G7 countries appear to have reached consensus on new sanctions targeting Russia's oil and gas exports, especially the shadow fleet, the CFR's Liana Fix reports. The US has also indicated it may not extend waivers created during the Iran war energy crisis that allowed the sale of Russian crude already at sea.

Russia's fossil fuel export revenues actually rose 2% in May to €726 million per day despite stable export volumes, driven by higher prices, according to CREA. China remained the top buyer at €7 billion, India increased imports to €5.8 billion, and Turkey imported €2.8 billion. Spain doubled its Russian LNG imports to €337 million.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is tightening the screws on Russia's oil evasion infrastructure at a moment when the Iran crisis has boosted Russian energy revenues. Whether the US actually lets those crisis-era Russian oil waivers lapse — and whether European nations actually enforce them against their own buyers — will determine if this round of sanctions has teeth.

sections.heading: Russia Forced to Import Gasoline by Sea as Ukrainian Drone Strikes Bite
sections.body:
Russia is set to import gasoline by sea this month as it manages a domestic fuel shortage caused by extensive Ukrainian drone attacks on its refineries, according to four industry sources cited by Reuters. The development is a reversal for one of the world's largest oil producers.

Ukraine struck key oil infrastructure in May, including the Tamanneftegaz terminal on the Black Sea, causing exports from the port of Taman to decline by 53% month-on-month, CREA reported. The terminal was struck again on June 13 in a joint SBU-SSO-HUR operation. The IEA confirmed that Ukrainian attacks have forced Russia to prioritize domestic fuel supply and maximize crude oil exports instead.

Russian crude and refined fuel exports remained stable at 7.4 million bpd in May, the IEA said, but the composition is shifting: more crude, less refined product, as damaged refineries reduce throughput.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign is reshaping Russia's fuel export profile in real time. A net oil importer for gasoline is a headline that matters politically — it shows the strikes are hitting Russia's domestic economy, not just its export revenue.

sections.heading: G7 Declares War on China's Rare Earth Dominance, Targets 60% Cap
sections.body:
The G7 issued a formal Leaders' Declaration on Securing Supply Chains for Critical Minerals at Évian, committing to reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier for rare earths and permanent magnets to under 60% by 2030, with an ambition to reach 50% 'as soon as possible.' Currently, China mines roughly 60% of the world's rare earths, processes about 90%, and manufactures over 95% of downstream permanent magnets.

Policy tools under discussion include price floors, tariffs, demand aggregation, and production alliances with partner countries. The declaration builds on last year's Critical Minerals Action Plan and the Critical Minerals Production Alliance launched under Canada's 2025 G7 presidency. Since early 2026, 195 projects totaling €64 billion in investment have been announced across G7 and partner countries. The declaration also announced a lithium and nickel traceability pilot, expanding to five additional minerals per year.

Separately, Paladin Envirotech announced a strategic initiative with South Korea's Daeheung M&T to deploy rare earth magnet recycling in Suwon — a first-of-its-kind deployment in the country — and expanded European operations in Helmond, Netherlands, bringing total global investment to $85 million.
sections.whyItMatters: For the first time, the G7 has put a number on decoupling from China's rare earth monopoly — 60% by 2030, 50% thereafter. The targets are ambitious given current Chinese dominance in processing (90%) and magnet manufacturing (95%). The question is whether price floors and offtake agreements can make non-Chinese supply chains competitive before Beijing weaponizes the dependency again.

sections.heading: Syria Emerges as Hormuz Bypass, Rewriting Regional Energy Corridors
sections.body:
Syria is pitching itself at the G7 as a safe Hormuz alternative for Europe and Gulf states, leveraging its Mediterranean ports and land routes. According to the Christian Science Monitor, some 1,400 Iraqi oil trucks per day are crossing Syria to reach the port of Baniyas, handling 90,000 barrels per day of Iraqi oil. The port, largely inactive for over a decade, is now being dredged to accept larger vessels.

DP World, an Emirati logistics company, manages Tartus port under a 30-year, $800 million deal signed in July 2025, and has increased its capacity tenfold — from 100,000 tons per month to 1 million tons per month as of April 2026. Latakia and Tartus report a 25% increase in goods since the Hormuz closure in March.

There are even talks of reviving the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline, a 621-mile line built in the 1950s to export Iraqi oil through the Mediterranean, destroyed during the Syrian civil war. Syrian Transport Minister Yarub Badr described Syria as a 'land bridge' that 'cannot be a complete replacement for maritime shipping' but represents 'an alternative corridor' in challenging times.

The irony: Tehran's closure of Hormuz has handed an economic lifeline to Syria's new government under Ahmed al-Sharaa, who ousted the Iran-backed Assad regime in December 2024.
sections.whyItMatters: Even if Hormuz reopens fully, the overland corridors through Syria have proven viable enough that Gulf states and European buyers are investing in them as permanent alternatives. This reshapes regional trade geography and gives post-Assad Syria unexpected economic leverage — partly funded by the UAE and partly by the same Iranian action that was supposed to cripple Gulf economies.

bottomLine: The US-Iran framework deal is real, but Hormuz won't flow freely for weeks — 1,500 ships are still waiting, mines need clearing, and Iran is already angling to extract 'service fees' from transit. Oil prices have dropped over a third from April peaks on the deal, but the IEA warns of a whipsaw: severe inventory draws now, followed by a potential 5 million bpd surplus in 2027. The G7 used the summit to tighten Russia's shadow fleet sanctions and set the first hard numerical targets for reducing China's rare earth dominance — 60% by 2030. Meanwhile, Syria has quietly emerged as an overland energy corridor, processing 90,000 bpd of Iraqi oil through Mediterranean ports, a shift that may outlast the Hormuz crisis itself.
sources: CFR, CSMonitor, Channel News Asia/IEA, NPR, Reuters, Kyiv Post/CREA, CryptoBriefing, G7 Leaders' Declaration (CNW), Paladin Envirotech/Business Wire, AOL/Moneywise, Daily Caller, Gulf Today
